explanation.title: Making Decisions with if, else if, and else

codeExample.title: Exam Grade Classifier
codeExample.explanation:
- `let grade, feedback;` — declares two `let` variables without assigning them yet; they will be set inside the branches
- `if (score >= 90)` — the parentheses hold the boolean condition; `>=` is greater-than-or-equal
- `} else if (score >= 80) {` — only evaluated when `score < 90`; once a branch matches, the rest are skipped
- `grade = "B";` — variables declared with `let` outside the block are still in scope inside it
- `if (promoCode)` — a non-empty string is truthy; if `promoCode` were `""` or `null`, the block would not run

exercise.title: Write an Age Category Classifier
exercise.instructions:
Write a program that classifies a person into an age category. Declare const age = 17. Use if/else if/else to assign a category string: under 13 is 'Child', 13 to 17 is 'Teenager', 18 to 59 is 'Adult', and 60 or above is 'Senior'. Print the age and the category on separate labelled lines.

Expected output with age = 17:
Age: 17
Category: Teenager
hints: 💡 Add else if (age <= 59) { category = "Adult"; } after the Teenager branch.
💡 The final category (Senior) needs only else { category = "Senior"; } because all lower ages are already handled.
💡 Each else if only needs to check the upper bound — the lower bound is guaranteed by the previous branch having been false.

questions.question: How many else if branches can a single if statement have in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: There is no limit on the number of else if branches. You can chain as many as your logic requires. JavaScript evaluates them top to bottom and executes the first branch whose condition is true. If none match, the optional else block runs.

questions.question: What does the grade classifier print when score = 85, given: if (score >= 90) A; else if (score >= 80) B; else C?
questions.explanation: JavaScript evaluates conditions top to bottom and stops at the first match. score >= 90 is false (85 < 90), so it moves to else if (score >= 80). Since 85 >= 80 is true, it prints B and skips the else. Only one branch executes in an if/else if/else chain.

explanation.title: Comparing Values and Combining Conditions
explanation.content:
# Comparison Operators

A condition inside an `if` statement evaluates to `true` or `false`. Comparison operators produce these boolean values by comparing two operands:

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `===` | Strict equal | `5 === 5` | `true` |
| `!==` | Strict not equal | `5 !== 3` | `true` |
| `>` | Greater than | `7 > 3` | `true` |
| `<` | Less than | `3 < 7` | `true` |
| `>=` | Greater or equal | `5 >= 5` | `true` |
| `<=` | Less or equal | `4 <= 3` | `false` |

Always use `===` and `!==` (strict), not `==` and `!=` (loose), to avoid coercion surprises.

## Logical Operators

Combine multiple conditions with logical operators:

| Operator | Meaning | Returns true when |
|---|---|---|
| `&&` | AND | Both operands are truthy |
| `\|\|` | OR | At least one operand is truthy |
| `!` | NOT | The operand is falsy |

```javascript
const age   = 25;
const hasId = true;

if (age >= 18 && hasId) {
    console.log("Access granted");
}
```

## Short-Circuit Evaluation

JavaScript evaluates `&&` and `||` lazily:

- `a && b` — if `a` is falsy, `b` is never evaluated
- `a || b` — if `a` is truthy, `b` is never evaluated

This is used idiomatically to provide default values:

```javascript
const username = inputName || "Guest";  // use "Guest" if inputName is falsy
const display  = user && user.name;     // safe — only reads .name if user exists
```

## The in Operator

```javascript
const plan = "pro";
const validPlans = ["starter", "pro", "bundle"];
if (validPlans.includes(plan)) {
    console.log("Valid plan.");
}
```

In the code example below, you will see logical operators used to build a complete access control check.

codeExample.title: Subscription Access Controller
codeExample.explanation:
- `isLoggedIn && accountActive && plan === "pro"` — all three conditions must be truthy for `hasFullAccess` to be `true`
- `hasFullAccess || trialDaysLeft > 0` — `true` if either condition is met; `||` stops at the first truthy value
- `!accountActive` — the `!` operator flips `true` to `false` and vice versa
- `"" || "Guest User"` — short-circuit default: the empty string is falsy so JavaScript evaluates and returns the right side
- `plan !== "free"` — strict not-equal; true for any plan string that is not exactly `"free"`

exercise.title: Build a Loan Eligibility Checker
exercise.instructions:
Write a program that checks loan eligibility using three variables: age (number), monthlyIncome (number), and hasExistingLoan (boolean). The criteria are: age must be between 21 and 60 inclusive, monthlyIncome must be at least 25000, and hasExistingLoan must be false. Print 'Eligible' if all criteria pass, otherwise print 'Not eligible'.

Test values: age=30, monthlyIncome=35000, hasExistingLoan=false.
Expected output: Eligible
hints: 💡 Use && to combine all conditions: age range, income threshold, and no existing loan.
💡 Check the age range with age >= 21 && age <= 60 — both bounds must hold simultaneously.
💡 Use !hasExistingLoan instead of hasExistingLoan === false — both work, but ! is more concise.

questions.question: What does the && operator return when the left operand is falsy?
options: The left operand without evaluating the right
questions.explanation: JavaScript's && operator uses short-circuit evaluation. If the left operand is falsy, JavaScript immediately returns that falsy value without evaluating the right operand at all. For example, null && someFunction() returns null and someFunction is never called. This is why && is used as a safe property access guard.

questions.question: What is the value of the expression: const name = '' || 'Anonymous'?
questions.explanation: The || operator returns the first truthy value it encounters, or the last value if none are truthy. An empty string '' is falsy, so JavaScript evaluates the right side and returns 'Anonymous'. This pattern is the standard JavaScript way to provide a default value when a variable might be falsy.

explanation.title: Concise Decisions with the Ternary Operator and switch

codeExample.title: Plan Feature Checker
codeExample.explanation:
- `userPlan === "bundle" ? "Full" : "Limited"` — evaluates the condition, returns `"Full"` when true, `"Limited"` when false
- `userScore >= 90 ? "A" : userScore >= 80 ? "B" : "C"` — a chained ternary; equivalent to nested `if/else if/else`
- `switch (userPlan)` — evaluates `userPlan` once and jumps to the matching `case` label
- `break` — required to exit the `switch` block; without it execution falls into the next case
- `nickname ?? "CodeGuru Learner"` — `??` returns the right side only when `nickname` is `null` or `undefined`

exercise.title: Build a Day Type Classifier with switch
exercise.instructions:
Write a program using a switch statement that classifies a day number (1=Mon through 7=Sun) into a type. Cases 1 and 2 should print 'Start of week', cases 3 and 4 print 'Mid week', case 5 prints 'End of week', and default (6 or 7) prints 'Weekend'. Then use a ternary to set isWorkday as true if dayNumber is 1-5, else false. Print the day number, day type, and isWorkday.

Test with dayNumber = 3. Expected output:
Day: 3
Type: Mid week
Work day: true
hints: 💡 Add case 5: dayType = "End of week"; break; after the Mid week block.
💡 Add default: dayType = "Weekend"; after case 5 — no break needed on the last case.
💡 The ternary is already written — it assigns true when dayNumber <= 5, otherwise false.

questions.question: What happens in a switch statement if you forget to add break at the end of a case?
options: Execution falls through and runs the next case's code too
questions.explanation: Without break, a switch statement falls through — after running the matched case's code, execution continues into the next case regardless of whether its label matches. This is occasionally useful (grouping multiple cases to share code), but usually a bug. Always add break unless fall-through is intentional.

questions.question: What is the difference between || and ?? for providing a default value?
options: They are completely identical in all situations
|| falls back when left is any falsy value; ?? falls back only when left is null or undefined
?? falls back when left is any falsy value; || only for null or undefined
|| works with strings; ?? works only with numbers
questions.explanation: || falls back when the left side is any falsy value (false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN). ?? (nullish coalescing) only falls back when the left side is null or undefined. This distinction matters when 0 or '' are valid values: count || 10 replaces a count of 0, but count ?? 10 keeps the 0 as intended.
